one_line_takeaway: FULM shows that decoupling unlearning from retention into separate LoRA adapters and merging them via a two-stage hierarchical strategy (TIES within similarity clusters, task-arithmetic summation across clusters) gives a better forgetting/utility trade-off than joint dual-objective unlearning or single-strategy adapter merging, under a federated setting with asymmetric client/server data access.
key_finding: In the near-iid TOFU setting (Table 1), FULM achieves Overall 72.13% versus KnOT 66.21%, SUM 64.93%, AVG 57.51%, and TIES 53.02%; FULM's Utility is 70.62% vs SUM's 56.92% (SUM amplifies unlearning but severely degrades utility), while TIES/AVG substantially hurt Real Authors/Real World retention. In the heterogeneous setting (Table 2: WMDP-Bio, WMDP-Cyber, MUSE-HP jointly merged), FULM reaches Overall 64.48% with Model Utility 57.25%, while SUM achieves comparable/stronger raw forgetting (Bio 27.46%, Cyber 34.47%, HP 29.31%) but at a much lower Utility (30.42%) and Overall (59.80%); AVG and TIES under-forget (Bio 59.85%/62.45%, Cyber 38.42%/41.87%) while keeping utility higher, illustrating the balance FULM strikes. For decoupling (Table 3, TOFU), FULM (using SUM to merge exactly one unlearning + one retention adapter) attains Overall 72.04% vs the centralized joint-training baseline GD's 66.68% and KnOT's 66.27%, with markedly lower Forget (20.79% vs GD's 53.57%) at comparable Utility (70.62% vs 67.16%), i.e., decoupled adapter training + merging outperformed jointly optimizing the dual unlearn/retain loss (Eq. 1) especially when the two datasets are similar/conflicting. Ablations (Table 4/8) show TIES is the better intra-cluster (near-iid) merge strategy (e.g., WMDP-Cyber TIES: Cyber 26.02%, Util 58.91% vs SUM: Cyber 26.62%, Util 23.90% -- SUM collapses utility via magnitude accumulation), whereas SUM is the better inter-cluster (heterogeneous) merge strategy (Table 5/9: SUM achieves Bio 39.05%/Cyber 25.82%/HP 34.48% forgetting at Util 57.25%, clearly stronger forgetting than Avg or TIES at similar utility) -- motivating the paper's hierarchical, strategy-per-similarity-level design. Additional appendix analyses show unlearning quality/utility degrade monotonically with more epochs (Table 6) and larger forget-set sizes (Table 7).
core_contribution: The paper proposes FULM (Federated UnLearning Merge), a federated-learning framework for unlearning undesirable knowledge from LLMs under asymmetric data access: unlearning-requesting clients (e.g., IP owners, red-teamers) hold the forget data but not retention data, while the server holding the model cannot access the sensitive forget data. FULM has two components: (1) 'split-and-merge' -- clients train unlearning adapters (LoRA) locally via task-specific objectives (RMU for WMDP, gradient ascent for TOFU/MUSE), decoupled from any retention objective, so unlearning and retention are learned as independent adapters rather than jointly optimized on a combined loss (Eq. 1); (2) a hierarchical, data-free merging strategy at the server: adapters are first clustered by cosine similarity of their parameter updates (near-iid/same-objective adapters cluster together), then merged within each cluster using a voting-based strategy (TIES) to reduce destructive interference among similar updates, and finally the cluster-level merged vectors are combined across clusters via simple task-arithmetic summation to preserve distinct, non-overlapping unlearning signals. This is a one-shot (single communication round) federated protocol, not iterative FL.
threat_model: Not an adversarial/attacker threat model in the security-attack sense; this is a data-governance/privacy-compliance setting. The 'threat' is unwanted retention of hazardous or sensitive knowledge (e.g., biosecurity/cybersecurity uplift knowledge from WMDP, copyrighted text from Harry Potter/MUSE, fictitious personal data from TOFU) inside a deployed LLM, motivated by privacy, copyright, and dual-use/hazardous-capability concerns. The paper explicitly does not assume a malicious client or server -- it assumes cooperative but privacy-constrained parties: the client (unlearning requester) cannot share its private forget data with the server, and the server cannot access the client's forget data due to privacy/security regulation, but data exchange of the trained LoRA adapters themselves is trusted. No poisoning, adapter-tampering, or Byzantine-client threat is modeled or evaluated.
limitations: The paper is an 8-page workshop paper (KDD'25 FedKDD workshop) with a single base model (Zephyr-7B) and no evaluation of robustness to malicious/Byzantine clients, adapter poisoning, or adversarial adapter submissions -- the federated setting is treated as privacy-motivated data separation, not an adversarial multi-party protocol. It is a one-shot (single-round) FL scheme, explicitly not evaluated under iterative multi-round federated dynamics. The similarity threshold ξ for clustering is introduced but its sensitivity/selection procedure is not analyzed in the main text. KNOT is excluded from the heterogeneous-domain comparison due to an architectural constraint (requires unified LoRA architecture for SVD), leaving an incomplete baseline comparison for that setting. No downstream re-learning/extraction attack is tested to verify unlearning is not merely surface-level (i.e., no adversarial probing for residual leakage of 'unlearned' knowledge). Results are on three specific benchmarks (WMDP, TOFU, MUSE) and may not generalize to other unlearning tasks or larger models.

datasets_used: WMDP (Biosecurity subset: 1,273 questions sourced from PubMed; Cybersecurity subset: 1,987 questions sourced from GitHub)
TOFU (fictitious-author QA unlearning benchmark, with Real Authors / Real World factual QA retention subsets)
MUSE (Harry Potter and the Goblet of Fire subset used as forget set)
MMLU (14,079 questions across 57 tasks, used as the Utility metric)
quantitative_results: Near-iid setting (Table 1, TOFU): FULM Overall 72.13% vs KnOT 66.21%, SUM 64.93%, AVG 57.51%, TIES 53.02%; FULM Utility 70.62% vs SUM Utility 56.92%
Heterogeneous setting (Table 2): FULM Overall 64.48%, Utility 57.25%, Bio 39.05%, Cyber 25.82%, HP 34.48%, vs SUM Overall 59.80%/Utility 30.42% and AVG/TIES Overall 54.34%/51.81%
Decoupling test (Table 3, TOFU): FULM(SUM) Overall 72.04%, Forget 20.79% vs GD (joint, centralized) Overall 66.68%, Forget 53.57%; KnOT Overall 66.27%
Intra-cluster ablation (Table 4a, WMDP-Cyber): TIES Cyber 26.02%/Util 58.91% vs SUM Cyber 26.62%/Util 23.90%
Intra-cluster ablation (Table 4b, WMDP-Bio): TIES Bio 41.32%/Util 57.95% vs SUM Bio 24.98%/Util 52.07%
Inter-cluster ablation (Table 5): SUM Bio 39.05%/Cyber 25.82%/HP 34.48%/Util 57.25%/Overall 64.48% vs Avg Overall 51.34% and TIES Overall 51.81%
Continued unlearning over epochs (Table 6, TOFU): Forget drops from 91.54% (epoch1) to 55.10% (epoch5) while Retain drops from 89.93% to 66.08% and Utility from 58.89% to 54.44%
Forget-set size (Table 7, TOFU): 25% forget-set size gives Utility 55.97%/Forget 71.23% vs 5% forget-set size Utility 57.16%/Forget 69.95%
baselines_compared: GD / Gradient Difference (centralized joint optimization of combined unlearn+retain loss, i.e., non-federated non-decoupled baseline)
